subject: ap-world-history
title: Islamic World & State Building
stimulus:
“The fall of the modern European empires was as rapid as their rise had been, and in most places the reasons
for their downfall were similar. Ultimately all [empires] had maintained their rule through [acceptance by
conquered peoples] rather than by force or the threat of force. . . . What had applied to the Roman Empire
also applied to the British and the French, the German, and, ultimately, even the Russian: subject peoples
were only to remain in subjection so long as at least a significant number of them could see some benefit to
them in so doing.
Resistance to any kind of rule requires organization and courage. In the case of resistance to colonial rule,
however, it also requires some vision of a better future in some postcolonial world. It requires an ideology
capable of mobilizing those who might otherwise be prepared to accept the status quo as merely inevitable.
Ironically, this ideology was provided by the same refashioning of society that had been the driving force
behind most modern imperialism, namely nationalism.”
Source: Anthony Pagden, British historian, Peoples and Empires, 2003

parts.prompt: Identify ONE claim that the author makes in the passage.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for identifying one historically defensible claim that the author makes in the passage.

required_elements: A specific claim drawn from the passage (not the student's own opinion).
alternatives.correct_example: The author claims that modern European empires fell rapidly.

required_elements: A specific claim drawn from the passage about the source of imperial rule or resistance.
alternatives.correct_example: The author claims that nationalism was the cause of resistance to colonial empires.

wrong_examples: A vague statement like 'the author talks about empires' without naming a specific claim.
Restating the prompt without quoting or paraphrasing a specific claim from the passage.
Student's own argument about imperialism instead of what Pagden actually argues.
common_traps: Summarizing the whole passage instead of picking ONE specific claim.
Confusing the author's claim with the student's own interpretation.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE claim that the author makes in the passage. The response must identify a specific claim, not restate the prompt or offer a vague reference.

sample_responses.response: The author claims that modern European empires fell rapidly.
sample_responses.commentary: A specific claim taken directly from the passage's opening line. Identifies Pagden's assertion about the rapidity of decolonization.

sample_responses.response: The author claims that nationalism was the cause of resistance to colonial empires.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies Pagden's central claim in the second paragraph about nationalism as the ideology of anti-colonial resistance.

sample_responses.response: One claim that the author makes is that subject peoples were willing to remain subjugated by a colonial power if a significant number of them saw benefits from such subjugation.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies Pagden's claim about acceptance rather than force being the basis of imperial rule.

sample_responses.response: The author talks about empires and how they worked.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not identify a specific claim from the passage.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE development in the second half of the twentieth century could be used to support
the author’s claim about nationalism in the second paragraph.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE development in the second half of the 20th century supports the author's claim about nationalism driving anti-colonial resistance.

required_elements: A specific named anti-colonial movement from the second half of the 20th century
Explanation connecting the movement to nationalist mobilization
alternatives.correct_example: The Indian independence movement was largely driven by nationalists who wanted to end British colonial rule.

required_elements: A specific named African or Asian nationalist leader/movement post-1945
Connection to Pagden's claim about nationalism as the mobilizing ideology
alternatives.correct_example: Nationalist resistance in Africa supports the author's claim because nationalist leaders such as Kwame Nkrumah led the struggle for independence based on self-governance and economic development.

wrong_examples: General reference to 'decolonization' without naming a specific movement or leader.
A development from the first half of the 20th century (e.g., pre-WWII) — outside the prompt's time window.
Naming a movement but not explaining how it reflects nationalism as a mobilizing ideology.
common_traps: Using the Indian independence movement but focusing on Gandhi's non-violence rather than nationalist mobilization.
Citing Cold War proxy conflicts without connecting them to anti-colonial nationalism.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE development in the second half of the twentieth century supports the author's claim about nationalism.

sample_responses.response: Nationalist sentiments increased in the second half of the twentieth century and led revolutionaries to overthrow governments.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the broader rise of nationalist resistance after WWII and explicitly connects it to government overthrow — supports Pagden's claim.

sample_responses.response: The Indian independence movement was largely driven by nationalists who wanted to end British colonial rule.
sample_responses.commentary: Specific named movement with an explanation of how nationalism motivated it — directly supports Pagden's claim about nationalism mobilizing colonial resistance.

sample_responses.response: The Vietnamese resistance movement against French rule was an example of nationalist resistance that supports the author's argument.
sample_responses.commentary: Specific example (Vietnam vs. France) that illustrates nationalism as the mobilizing ideology — aligns with Pagden's second paragraph.

sample_responses.response: Nationalist resistance in Africa supports the author's claim because nationalist leaders such as Kwame Nkrumah led the struggle for independence based on self-governance and economic development.
sample_responses.commentary: Names a specific leader (Nkrumah) and movement (Ghanaian independence) and explicitly connects to nationalist mobilization — clear support for the claim.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE development from the nineteenth or twentieth century could be used to challenge
a claim that the author makes in the first paragraph.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE 19th- or 20th-century development challenges a claim Pagden makes in the first paragraph (about empire maintained through acceptance rather than force).

required_elements: A specific 19th- or 20th-century example of colonial resistance
Explanation that shows the claim about acceptance/benefits is weakened
alternatives.correct_example: The author's claim is challenged by the many examples of colonial resistance to imperial rule.

required_elements: A specific case of colonial powers retaining colonies by force into the late 20th century
Explanation challenging the 'rapid fall' claim in the first paragraph
alternatives.correct_example: The author's claim is challenged by examples of European colonial powers managing to retain colonies well into the 1970s or 1980s (for example, the Portuguese maintaining control of Angola or Mozambique).

wrong_examples: General reference to 'colonialism was bad' without a specific 19th- or 20th-century development.
Citing an event but not explaining how it challenges a specific claim from paragraph 1.
Using nationalism itself — which supports rather than challenges Pagden's argument.
common_traps: Students often describe resistance without explicitly tying it to Pagden's claim that rule depended on acceptance.
Citing decolonization as a challenge when it actually supports the 'rapid fall' claim.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE development from the nineteenth or twentieth century could be used to challenge a claim that the author makes in the first paragraph.

sample_responses.response: The author's claim is challenged by the many examples of colonial resistance to imperial rule.
sample_responses.commentary: Challenges the claim that empires rested on acceptance — resistance shows force was often needed. Credited when elaborated with specific resistance examples.

sample_responses.response: The author's claim is challenged by examples of European colonial powers managing to retain colonies well into the 1970 or 1980s (for example, the Portuguese maintaining control of Angola or Mozambique).
sample_responses.commentary: Directly challenges Pagden's 'rapid fall' thesis by citing specific long-lasting colonial holdings (Portuguese Africa).

sample_responses.response: Relatively few colonial subjects welcomed invitations to integrate politically with the imperial 'motherland' or become full citizens (as demonstrated by the case of Senegal or Algeria in the French Empire).
sample_responses.commentary: Challenges Pagden's acceptance-through-benefits claim — French assimilation policies largely failed, indicating empires were NOT broadly accepted by colonial subjects.

sample_responses.response: There were many events in the 20th century.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not identify a specific development or explain how it challenges Pagden's claim.
